🫐 The UK Fresh Blueberry Market

Britain's blueberry year

Britain eats blueberries every week of the year — yet almost none are grown here in winter. Behind every punnet is a quiet global relay. Pick an exporter and an importer below to read that trade from either seat.

An automatic, self-updating read of HMRC trade records and Chilean customs data · refreshed 25 June 2026

Global → UKHMRC OTS

The relay

Who lands when — average tonnes per month across recent years.

The year opens in the southern-hemisphere summer: Chile and Peru carry January and February. As they fade, Morocco floods spring — its April peak is the single biggest month any origin posts all year. A midsummer lull follows, the one window when British and near-European fruit matters most. Then the baton swings back across the equator: Peru and South Africa ramp hard through the autumn to close the year.

Global → UKHMRC OTS

Who supplies Britain

Share of everything that landed in the last twelve months.

No single country owns the British blueberry. Peru and Morocco run neck-and-neck at the top, together better than half the market; South Africa is the clear third. Chile — the origin this project tracks weekly, fruit by fruit — is smaller by tonnage but lands in the highest-value winter window.

Global → UKHMRC + Chile DUS

What's on Britain's shelves now

The shelf labels neither origin nor variety — but the import flow does. This is the mix landing right now, and the cultivars inside Chile's share.

A UK punnet carries no cultivar and a rotating, often-unlabelled origin, so "what's offered" can't be read off the shelf — you read it off the customs flow. The upper bar is the origin mix of fruit landing in the latest month (April 2026), the relay's current hand; the lower bar is the cultivar split within Chile's shipments — Legacy leads, then Duke, with premium Blue Ribbon and Draper behind — the only stage where variety is ever recorded.

Global → UKHMRC OTS

What it costs

Average import price per kilo, by month of the year.

The landed price of an imported kilo hovers around £5.66, but it is not flat. It firms in early autumn — as the southern season restarts and air-freighted early fruit commands a premium — and softens through the high-volume mid-winter and late-spring gluts. Price tracks scarcity, not the calendar.

Global → UKComtrade + HMRC

What every origin charges — and the freight wedge

Two prices per supplier: what the fruit costs leaving its own country (FOB) and what it's worth landed in Britain (CIF). The gap is mostly ocean freight.

Same fruit, two ends of the journey. The grey dot is each origin's own export price; the purple dot is what Britain pays for it at the border (2025). The distance between them is the freight-and-insurance wedge — wide for the deep-sea origins (Chile and South Africa carry roughly £0.7–1.7/kg of shipping), slim for near-Europe. Where the wedge goes negative the country is a re-export hub, not a true grower — the Netherlands' "exports" are largely fruit landed elsewhere first, so its FOB sits above the UK border price. A clean tell for which origins actually grow what they ship. (Lanes under ~250 t/yr are dropped — Comtrade revises their unit values too heavily.)

Chile → UKChile DUS customs

What Chile ships

The varieties inside Chile's punnets, by volume sent to the UK.

Chilean customs records name the cultivar on most shipments, so we can see exactly which berries Britain buys. Legacy dominates — a firm, travel-hardy variety bred for exactly this kind of six-week sea journey — followed by Duke and the premium club varieties Blue Ribbon and Draper.

Chile → UKCatastro Frutícola

What Chile is planting next

The orchard census, read by planting year — which varieties Chile is putting in the ground, and which it's quietly abandoning.

Today's punnet is yesterday's planting decision, so the census is a forward look. The old workhorses are aging out: Legacy and Duke still dominate the ground, but almost none has been planted since 2019, and Brigitta/Elliot are pure legacy. The replant is going to the premium club varieties — Blue Ribbon barely existed before 2010 and is now the standout of recent plantings, with Suziblue and Top Shelf behind. As those young blocks reach full bearing, the mix Britain buys will tilt the same way — better-travelling, higher-value fruit, the move Chile is making to answer Peru.

Chile → GlobalUN Comtrade

Where in the world to sell it

Flip the seat. You grow in Chile — where does a kilo net the most?

The same customs data, read the other way, prices every market Chile ships to. After deducting ocean freight, a kilo sent to South Korea nets a grower roughly 28% more than the same kilo into the United States — and the premium survives the longer voyage. Yet the US alone takes 41% of Chilean fruit, because the premium lanes are tiny. Holland looks cheap, but it is a re-export hub — a distribution valve, not a final table.

Peru → GlobalUN Comtrade

Where Peru sells — and its one big risk

The same lens on the world's #1 exporter. Peru ships ~360k t a season — but to a far narrower set of tables than Chile.

Peru's reach is strikingly concentrated: the United States alone takes about 56% of its blueberries, and the Netherlands — Europe's re-export valve — another 22%. Asia is still a small slice but the fastest-growing: Hong Kong, China, Singapore and India all jumped double- or triple-digit year on year. The flip side of dominance is exposure — over half the crop rides one destination, so a US price wobble or trade-policy shift lands harder on Peru than on anyone, Chile included.

Peru → GlobalUSDA-FAS

Peru's supply outlook

Peru has no orchard census like Chile's — so the USDA's annual estimate is the structural and forward view: how much is planted, picked, and shipped, two seasons out.

The trajectory is relentless: harvested area near 22,000 ha and production forecast to a record 355k t in 2025/26, with exports around 335k t. The striking part is the constancy of the concentration — the United States has taken ~54–56% of Peru's fruit every year, and the USDA forecast has that share edging up to 56%. Peru isn't diversifying away from its one big customer; it's doubling down while Asia grows underneath.

Peru → UKHMRC + Comtrade

Peru → Britain

When Peru supplies British shelves, and at what price.

Peru's arrivals into the UK peak around Oct. It lands at about £5.82/kg CIF, from an export price near $6.65/kg FOB (2024) — a freight wedge of about £0.77/kg.

Morocco → UKHMRC + Comtrade

Morocco → Britain

When Morocco supplies British shelves, and at what price.

Morocco's arrivals into the UK peak around Apr. It lands at about £6.20/kg CIF, from an export price near $5.85/kg FOB (2024) — a freight wedge of about £1.75/kg.

South Africa → UKHMRC + Comtrade

South Africa → Britain

When South Africa supplies British shelves, and at what price.

South Africa's arrivals into the UK peak around Nov. It lands at about £7.19/kg CIF, from an export price near $5.32/kg FOB (2025) — a freight wedge of about £3.15/kg.

Spain → UKHMRC + Comtrade

Spain → Britain

When Spain supplies British shelves, and at what price.

Spain's arrivals into the UK peak around May. It lands at about £6.59/kg CIF, from an export price near $8.10/kg FOB (2025) — a freight wedge of about £0.44/kg.

Netherlands → UKHMRC + Comtrade

Netherlands → Britain

When Netherlands supplies British shelves, and at what price.

Netherlands's arrivals into the UK peak around Jan. It lands at about £5.22/kg CIF, from an export price near $6.28/kg FOB (2025) — a freight wedge of about £0.45/kg.

Chile → ChinaSAG + Comtrade

Who can actually chase the premium

The best price means nothing if your fruit can't get in. China only admits SAG-registered orchards — so which of our named producers are cleared?

Matching our named Chilean exporters against the SAG roster of orchards authorised for China, 23 of 72 producers in our flow are cleared — covering 66% of the UK-bound volume we can name. Those are the growers positioned to steer fruit toward the $1.06/kg premium that Asia nets over the US bulk lane. The rest are locked to the Atlantic markets until they clear registration.

Chile → UKHMRC + DUS · model

How we know what's coming — two weeks early

Chile's export records publish weeks before Britain's official import figures. By transit-shifting the outbound shipments, we estimate each month's Chilean arrivals about two weeks before HMRC prints them — and the call beats simply assuming "same as last year".

~2 wks
ahead of the official figure
60%
got the direction right

better than the naive baseline
35
in-season calls back-tested
No dedicated blocks for this pair yet — the coverage grid above shows where the data currently sits. This is where new sources slot in.
Sources & honesty. Volumes and prices: HMRC Overseas Trade Statistics (fresh blueberries, 08104050), reconciled across vintages. Chilean detail: Aduana DUS customs records. Destination & origin prices: UN Comtrade (HS 081040). China access: SAG orchard roster. Each block is stamped with its source; the two-week edge is validated out-of-sample on the Chilean lane only. The discipline is the product.
